scope: Requirement lifecycle: spec req, spec supersede, spec amend, and @-refs. Belongs here: next-id allocation; non-interactive authoring; supersede / move / amend; @path file-ref resolution. Does not belong here: scaffolding a whole domain (spec domain new → DOMAIN). Boundary case: minting a requirement id is REQ, scaffolding the domain that holds it is DOMAIN.

requirements.statement: spec req <domain-prefix> resolves its prefix case-insensitively against the domains found on the filesystem, uppercasing the input and stripping whitespace before matching.
requirements.why: Requiring the exact uppercased key before an id can be minted is friction with no safety gain; resolving case-insensitively against the real domain listing removes it without guessing a domain.

requirements.statement: spec req <domain-prefix> resolves to the domain whose key exactly equals the input when one exists, and otherwise to the single domain the input is a unique prefix of.
requirements.why: Without exact-match precedence, `auth` would be ambiguous whenever both AUTH and AUTHX exist, blocking the author from the domain they plainly named.

requirements.statement: spec req <domain-prefix> prints the matching candidate domains to stderr and exits 2 when the prefix matches more than one domain and none matches exactly.
requirements.why: Silently choosing one of several matching domains would mint the id in the wrong domain — a requirement authored into BOOKING when the author meant BILLING is a durable mistake.

requirements.statement: spec req <domain-prefix> prints the available domains to stderr and exits 2 when the prefix matches no existing domain, and never mints a <KEY>-001 id for a domain that does not exist.
requirements.why: Minting an id for a non-existent domain would create a requirement with no SPEC.json to hold it; domain creation belongs to spec domain new, kept separate so a typo cannot silently birth a domain.

requirements.statement: spec req <domain-prefix>, run non-interactively with piped stdin, prints the next unused id — the domain's SPEC.json max sequence + 1, padded to three digits, as <KEY>-<NNN> on stdout — and exits 0.
requirements.why: IDs are never reused; the id query must agree with the domain envelope's requirements, not a regex approximation.

requirements.statement: spec req on a non-platform directory prints the friendly not-a-platform message and exits 2.
Exit codes across the authoring surfaces are 0 and 2 only; exit 1 stays reserved for spec check --ci semantic failures.
requirements.why: CI must be able to distinguish gate failures from authoring/usage errors.

requirements.statement: When stdin is a TTY, spec req enters the interactive per-field prompt flow with prompts rendered to stderr; the allocated id and Active status are displayed but not editable.
requirements.why: stdout stays machine-parseable while humans author interactively.

requirements.statement: When stdin is not a TTY, spec req prints the next unused id and exits 0 — no prompts, no writes, no readline.
requirements.why: The composable id query for scripts and agents is preserved byte-for-byte.

requirements.statement: spec req, in its interactive TTY flow, aborts with a stderr notice and exit 0 writing zero bytes when the author submits an empty Requirement statement.
requirements.why: Following the git-editor convention, an abandoned authoring session must be a clean no-op — treating an empty statement as a save would persist a meaningless entry.

requirements.statement: spec req <KEY> --text appends a new Active requirement to the domain's SPEC.json — status active, the given statement, why null, empty relates/livesIn/issues, changedAtVersion 1 — and bumps the envelope's updated to the local date, such that the appended entry re-indexes with zero diagnostics.
requirements.why: Authoring must produce exactly what the indexer ingests, or authored requirements silently vanish.

requirements.statement: An @-ref is @<relative/path> preceded by start-of-string or whitespace, containing at least one slash, resolved against the platform root; emails, @spec tags, and version pins never match.
requirements.why: Requirement text points at code without false positives on everyday @ usage.

requirements.statement: spec req scans each authored field for an @-prefixed relative-path code reference and, when that path does not resolve under the platform root, warns on stderr while still saving the requirement.
requirements.why: A requirement may legitimately point at code that does not exist yet; blocking the save on an unresolved reference would stop authors from recording intent ahead of implementation.

requirements.statement: spec term authors, lists, and revises glossary TERM entries (definition in the statement, headword in term, synonyms in aliases), and spec supersede/amend operate on TERM ids exactly as on requirements — carrying term/aliases through the successor and amend shapes.
requirements.why: Terms are lifecycle-managed like requirements: a shift in meaning must supersede (or revise-with-bump), never silently redefine; reusing the one lifecycle machinery keeps a term and a requirement governed by the same guarantees.

requirements.statement: spec amend refuses any requirement that has at least one bound @spec tag; amend is permitted only for a Draft entry or an Active entry with zero bound tags, and once code tags a requirement, spec supersede is the sole path that may change it.
requirements.why: amend rewrites a requirement in place with no version bump, so amending a requirement that code already binds would silently mutate shipped truth out from under the @spec tags that verify it, leaving no supersede trail recording that the meaning changed.

requirements.statement: spec supersede and spec move take every version they emit — the --json spec_version for supersede, the source and target versions for move, and the persisted supersededAtVersion died-at stamp — from the freshly-reindexed supersede DAG, never from an incremented authored envelope counter.
requirements.why: If these commands emitted or stamped a self-incremented authored number, the version a lineage records and an agent parses would diverge from the DAG-derived number that pins, drift, and gate compare against, resurrecting the two-sources-of-truth split the derived model exists to close.

requirements.statement: spec req <KEY> --text appends a new Active requirement to the domain's SPEC.json — status active, the given statement, why null when unset, empty relates/livesIn/issues, and no authored version counter — and bumps the envelope's updated to the local date, such that the appended entry re-indexes with zero diagnostics.
requirements.why: Authoring must produce exactly what the indexer ingests, or authored requirements silently vanish.
